name: Mourning
shortDescription: Mourning is a concept deeply intertwined with Egyptian funerary rites and beliefs about the afterlife, often personified in goddesses like Isis and Nephthys.
description: In Egyptian mythology, mourning is not typically represented by a single, distinct deity with a specific name like many other gods and goddesses. Instead, the concept of mourning is intrinsically linked to funerary rites and the transition to the afterlife. The profound sorrow and lamentation expressed during funerary ceremonies were seen as crucial for the deceased's successful journey into the Duat. Goddesses like Isis and Nephthys frequently embody the essence of mourning. Isis, through her tireless search and grief over the death of her husband Osiris, exemplifies the most potent form of mourning. Nephthys, despite being the wife of Set, also mourned Osiris alongside Isis, highlighting the universality of grief and the shared pain of loss. Their wails and lamentations were considered essential for ensuring Osiris's resurrection and the continuation of cosmic order. The depictions of Isis and Nephthys flanking sarcophagi or canopic jars symbolize their role as protectors and mourners of the deceased. The presence of professional mourners was also a significant aspect of Egyptian funerals. These individuals, both men and women, would perform elaborate rituals of wailing, tearing their hair, and beating their breasts to express the community's grief and honor the deceased. These performances were believed to facilitate the deceased's passage into the afterlife by attracting the attention of the gods and ensuring the proper performance of funerary rites.

sources.title: Myths and Symbols of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Lucia Gahlin
